title: Zaccharie Risacher Records First Career DNP As Hawks Tighten Rotation Amid Playoff Push
summary: Zaccharie Risacher, the first overall pick in the 2024 NBA Draft, was a healthy scratch for the first time in his career Wednesday as the Atlanta Hawks fell 122-116 to the Cleveland Cavaliers, spending his 21st birthday on the bench for the duration. The development marks a significant shift for the young French forward, who started the first two-thirds of the season before losing his spot in the lineup following a 128-97 home loss to the Miami Heat that dropped Atlanta to 27-31. Since CJ McCollum replaced Risacher in the starting five, the Hawks have gone 18-4 and climbed to fifth in the Eastern Conference. The arrival of Jonathan Kuminga further complicated Risacher's situation. The two players occupy similar positional roles, and with Kuminga now at full strength following a knee injury, Atlanta rarely deploys both simultaneously. Risacher played a career-low seven minutes in Monday's loss to New York before being ruled out entirely Wednesday, with Corey Kispert inserted into the rotation ahead of him. Quin Snyder has been careful to frame Risacher's diminished role as circumstantial rather than performance-based. "I think Zach's been doing all the things that I've talked about, that we've asked," Snyder said. "We only have so many guys that we can actually put in the game. JK played a little more, so those minutes come from somewhere. It's not anything about one player. That's just the game." Snyder added: "We need him to keep doing the things that he's been doing. I mean, he's great. He's been crashing the glass. Those are big plays for us." Atlanta's two most recent opponents, Cleveland and the New York Knicks, are also the franchise's most probable first-round playoff foes. With the rotation tightening around that reality, a near-term path back to consistent minutes for Risacher appears narrow.

title: Michael Malone Initially Turned Down North Carolina With Focus On NBA Return
summary: Michael Malone repeatedly declined North Carolina's overtures before agreeing to become the program's head coach, revealing Tuesday that he initially assumed the Tar Heels' contact was little more than a formality. When incoming athletic director Steve Newmark first reached out, Malone told him college basketball was not a serious consideration. He was focused on returning to the NBA following his dismissal from the Denver Nuggets and viewed the UNC inquiry as a courtesy call rather than a genuine pursuit. "Every time I said no, I was regretting that," Malone said at his introductory press conference. "I thought, I think I'm making a mistake." Malone stayed in contact with Newmark despite his hesitation, continuing to ask questions and leaving the door open. The situation shifted at the Final Four, when Arizona's Tommy Lloyd and Michigan's Dusty May both removed themselves from consideration. With their top alternatives gone, Newmark and director of basketball operations Eric Hoots flew from Indianapolis to Malone's home in Colorado on Easter Sunday to make a direct appeal. Malone's initial reaction to that request was also resistance. "A lot longer than we wanted him there," Malone joked of the eventual five-hour meeting. "My first reaction when they said they wanted to fly from Indianapolis was like, no ... but I'm so happy that they did come, because it changed my mind and changed my wife's mind - and that night I felt so much better about it." Additional factors helped close the deal. Malone's daughter Bridget, a member of UNC's volleyball team, told her father directly that she wanted him to take the position. His late father, Brendan, a longtime coach, had been a lifelong admirer of UNC and Dean Smith. Malone signed a six-year, $50 million contract with the Tar Heels .

title: Anthony Davis Details Shock Of Learning About Luka Doncic Trade, Says Lakers Showed Him No Respect
summary: Anthony Davis revealed he had no advance knowledge of the trade that sent him from the Los Angeles Lakers to the Dallas Mavericks in exchange for Luka Doncic, describing the night of Feb. 1, 2025 as one of complete disbelief during an appearance on "The Draymond Green Show" released Wednesday. Davis said his agent, Klutch Sports CEO Rich Paul, was the first to inform him of the deal. His initial reaction was skepticism. "I said, who the f- they gonna trade me for? Luka?" Davis said. "I was like, 'Who else could you trade me for on that team?'" While still on the phone with Paul, then-Mavericks general manager Nico Harrison called Davis directly. "I told you I was going to come get you one day. I wanted you," Harrison told him, according to Davis. Davis replied: "Huh?" Dallas sent Doncic to the Lakers for Davis, Max Christie and a 2029 unprotected first-round pick in the deal , which came just eight months after the Mavericks reached the NBA Finals. "I had no idea this was happening," Davis said. "And I couldn't make sense of it. From a business standpoint, I can't be mad. But why? It just caught me off guard." Davis made his Mavericks debut on Feb. 8, 2025, suffering a left adductor strain in his first game. He dealt with four separate injuries during his year in Dallas before being traded to the Washington Wizards in February. He has appeared in just 20 games this season and has been sidelined since January with a left hand injury. Davis, who won a championship alongside LeBron James in Los Angeles in 2020, expressed frustration over how his departure was handled after nearly six seasons with the franchise. "I still think I deserved much more respect than that," Davis said. "All the time I've been here. S-, I was there six years. We won a championship. All this stuff, and I can't get a phone call or a text of, 'This is what we're thinking.'"

title: NBA Investigating Kings' Foul On Seth Curry, Sources Say It Was Coaching Mistake, Not Tanking
summary: The NBA is reviewing a late-game decision by Sacramento Kings coach Doug Christie during Tuesday's 110-105 loss to the Golden State Warriors, with Kings sources telling ESPN the move was a tactical miscalculation rather than a deliberate effort to lose. With Sacramento leading by one and 3:15 remaining, Christie called for forward Doug McDermott to foul Seth Curry despite the Kings being in the penalty. Sources said Christie miscalculated that Golden State was in the bonus, sending Curry, an 86.4% career free throw shooter, to the line when more favorable fouling targets were available. Christie's intent, sources said, was to use a timeout before automatically losing it when the clock dropped below three minutes. Curry converted one of two free throws, tying the game at 101. Christie then drew up a successful out-of-timeout play for a McDermott three-pointer, putting Sacramento back up by three after the team had trailed by 16. The Kings ultimately surrendered that lead in the closing minutes. The decision drew pointed public criticism from Warriors forward Draymond Green, who connected it to the league's broader tanking concerns. "I saw a team tonight foul Seth Curry with three minutes to go for no reason," Green said. "I get fined when I do wrong. Fine the hell out of people." Sacramento dropped to 21-59 with the loss, tied with the Utah Jazz for the league's fourth-worst record. The Kings have been without Domantas Sabonis, Zach LaVine and De'Andre Hunter due to season-ending surgeries, with DeMar DeRozan and Russell Westbrook also inactive Tuesday. Despite an organizational awareness that finishing near the bottom improves their lottery position in a strong draft class, team sources were firm that Christie and the players on the floor were competing to win. "Tanking is the last thing I'd do," Christie said after a recent win. "I respect the game too much."

title: NBA Executives, Coaches Acknowledge Tanking Widely Disliked But Remains Most Effective Rebuilding Strategy
summary: Despite near-universal criticism from players, coaches and front office personnel, tanking has become the dominant team-building strategy for bottom-tier NBA franchises, with league sources acknowledging it will persist until the league establishes firm rules or meaningful penalties to stop it. ESPN spoke with dozens of individuals across the league and found broad agreement on the problem, if not the solution. Golden State Warriors coach Steve Kerr offered the most direct assessment. "I hate it," Kerr said. The on-court consequences have been stark. The average margin of victory in NBA games this season stands at 13.1 points, the largest in league history. A record 89 games have been decided by 30 or more points. Yet even those who find the practice troubling acknowledge its effectiveness. One Western Conference general manager detailed the scope of tactics currently in use. "These teams are doing the whole gamut: sitting guys in the fourth, playing analytically bad lineups, drawing up plays for bad shots," the executive said. "The creativity is impressive and I don't blame them. It's the best strategy to get better. Look at all the most promising teams in the league: Thunder, Spurs, Pistons, Rockets, Hornets. Years of being bad and building up on high picks. It's painful but worthwhile." The results of successful rebuilds have reinforced the strategy's appeal. The Oklahoma City Thunder, San Antonio Spurs and Detroit Pistons each endured prolonged losing stretches before emerging as legitimate contenders built around high lottery selections. Sources across the league agree the practice will continue in varying forms across different seasons and draft classes until the NBA establishes either structural rule changes or financial consequences severe enough to alter front office behavior. Commissioner Adam Silver has pledged a resolution before June , with new incentive structures targeted for implementation ahead of the 2026 draft.

title: Victor Wembanyama Ruled Out Wednesday With Rib Contusion, Optimism For Friday Return
summary: San Antonio Spurs center Victor Wembanyama and guard Stephon Castle will not play Wednesday against the Portland Trail Blazers, the team announced. Wembanyama is dealing with a rib contusion while Castle is sidelined with right knee soreness. The absences carry particular weight for Wembanyama, who needs at least 20 minutes in one more game to reach the NBA's required minimum of 65 games for individual award eligibility. San Antonio has three games remaining, including Wednesday's contest. The Spurs face the Dallas Mavericks on Friday and Denver Nuggets on Sunday, and the team expressed optimism that both players could return for the Friday matchup. Both participated in shootaround Wednesday morning. The Spurs will be off nearly an entire week before the NBA playoffs begin on April 18th. "I can't tell you too much of how [Wembanyama] looked, but he heals fast," veteran forward Harrison Barnes said. Wembanyama sustained the rib injury during Monday's 115-102 win over the Philadelphia 76ers, playing just under 16 minutes before exiting. That appearance counted as an official game under NBA guidelines, which permit two exceptions for games played between 15 and 19 minutes and 59 seconds. He finished with 17 points, five rebounds and three blocks in that outing. Castle recorded a triple-double in the same game with 17 points, 13 assists and 10 rebounds. San Antonio sits at 60-19, having clinched the Southwest Division title and a top-two seed in the Western Conference. The Spurs trail the Oklahoma City Thunder by three games in the conference standings .

title: Cade Cunningham To Return From Collapsed Lung Wednesday Against Bucks
summary: Detroit Pistons guard Cade Cunningham is expected to play Wednesday against the Milwaukee Bucks, sources told ESPN, marking his return from a collapsed left lung that sidelined him for 11 games. Cunningham suffered the injury March 17 against the Washington Wizards. Initial assessments suggested he would miss the remainder of the regular season and only ramp up during the week before the playoffs open April 18. Instead, Cunningham recovered ahead of schedule and is available for Detroit's final three regular-season contests after appearing on the injury report as doubtful in recent days. In 61 games this season, Cunningham is posting 24.5 points, 9.9 assists, 5.6 rebounds and 1.5 steals per game in 34.4 minutes. Detroit thrived during his absence, going 8-3 and clinching home-court advantage throughout the Eastern Conference playoffs on Saturday. His injury has carried implications beyond the court. The National Basketball Players Association is now pushing to eliminate the 65-game minimum requirement for postseason award eligibility, citing Cunningham's situation as a breaking point for a rule the union accepted three years ago.

title: Doc Rivers Hints At Retirement, Cites Grandchildren As Bucks' Disappointing Season Winds Down
summary: Milwaukee Bucks head coach Doc Rivers indicated Tuesday his time on an NBA sideline may be drawing to a close, citing family priorities as his contract situation and the team's uncertain future hang over the franchise. Rivers, whose deal runs through next season, made the remarks when asked how long he envisioned continuing to coach. "I won't answer that, but I have grandkids that I want to see," Rivers told reporters. "I have seven grandkids now, and they're all 8 years and under, and it kills me every time I miss grandparents day with each one of them in school. It's probably time to go see them more, so I'll let you figure out the rest." It marked the second such comment in a week. Before Milwaukee's March 31 win over Dallas, Rivers reflected on his tenure with a tone that struck reporters as valedictory. "When I was brought here two years ago, 2 1/2 years ago, I was brought into here to take the team to the next level and that just never happened, never materialized," Rivers said. "From a coaching perspective, you feel like the city that you're from, you didn't get the job done, and that is something I carry very heavy with me." Rivers took over mid-season in 2023-24, replacing Adrian Griffin. Milwaukee has posted a sub-.500 record since and will miss the playoffs for the first time since 2016. Despite the struggles, GM Jon Horst praised Rivers' work during a difficult year. "I do know it was an incredibly tough year and, in my opinion, Doc did his absolute best to keep the train on the rails as long as possible and I think it was kind of masterful in a lot of ways," Horst said. "He was unbelievable to work with throughout the year with it." Rivers has accumulated the sixth-most regular-season victories in NBA coaching history across 27 seasons, including a championship with Boston in 2008. Rivers was recently voted into the Hall of Fame.

title: NBA Could Have 8-12 Head Coaching Changes In Offseason
summary: The NBA had just two coaching changes during the 2025 offseason, but there is a growing expectation that the league could have more than a third of its teams turnover their position this year. "There's going to be 8 to 12 [changes]," one league source with direct knowledge of the coaching market told Jake Fischer . Last season, a pair of teams made sudden coaching changes right before the playoffs as the Memphis Grizzlies fired Taylor Jenkins and the Denver Nuggets fired Michael Malone. Their interim head coach hires Tuomas Iisalo and David Adelman were retained. Doug Christie and Mitch Johnson were also retained by the Sacramento Kings and San Antonio Spurs, respectively, after they initially were on an interim basis. This left the New York Knicks replacing Tom Thibodeau with Mike Brown, and the Phoenix Suns replacing Mike Budenholzer with Jordan Ott as the only true coaching changes in the offseason. The New Orleans Pelicans fired Willie Green midseason and James Borrego has led the team on an interim basis. Tiago Splitter has also served as interim head coach this season while Chauncey Billups has been on leave following his arrest as part of a federal gambling investigation. Doc Rivers has openly talked about the possibility of leaving the Milwaukee Bucks, while Jamahl Mosley's status with the Orlando Magic is very uncertain after a disappointing season. Steve Kerr is also entering the offseason without a contract for the 2026-27 season with the Golden State Warriors.

title: Jon Horst Says No Decision Has Been Made By Bucks On Giannis Antetokounmpo's Future
summary: Milwaukee Bucks general manager Jon Horst said Tuesday the franchise has not determined whether to pursue a contract extension or trade for Giannis Antetokounmpo, framing both options as equally viable heading into a pivotal offseason. Horst spoke to reporters in New York before Milwaukee's game against the Brooklyn Nets. The Bucks will miss the playoffs for the first time since the 2015-16 season. When pressed on whether the organization was operating with a clear direction on Antetokounmpo, Horst pushed back on the premise. "The lack of clarity, the lack of understanding my role, lack of fit - is that because it exists and there isn't any clarity, no conversation, no directness? Or is it because we're not winning?" Horst said. "There's no singular hero or knight riding in on the horse that saves the day and there's no singular villain. It's all of us in it together trying to figure it out." Horst acknowledged the organization holds meaningful assets, three tradable first-round picks, flexible contracts, and a high lottery selection, but stopped short of committing to any specific course. "I don't know what we're gonna do, and I don't have to," said Horst. "Like, we don't have to. What Wes (Edens) said is - you can take it however you want to take it - but what he said, in most cases, is accurate, right? In most cases, you either - whether you have an actual commitment and you offer an actual extension, it's way more about sentimentality and alignment and togetherness and path. You either have an alignment and an understanding and, not commitment necessarily, but a path forward together that you understand and you go execute it. You could call that an extension, or you try to figure out the best win-win that you possibly can moving forward via a trade. "It's not actually the only two doors, right? There's door No. 3 that actually exists, but that's just a level-set, non-emotional reality of where it's at on Oct. 1. And I'm not saying that you do something beforehand. I'm just saying you do have to understand where you want to go, but we don't have to know that today. "Yeah, we have three tradable firsts , right? We have tradable contracts. We have one of the best players in the world - the best player in the world, in my opinion - under contract. We have a high draft pick this year that hopefully it's a great opportunity to have a great pick in a great draft. We have to get that right. We either have to draft the right player and have that be a big part of how we go forward and compete, or we have to execute a trade using the value of that. "And the truth is I don't know," continued Horst. "We don't know what we're going to do today, and we don't have to. What I do think we have to do is be very honest, very direct, make a big assessment. We learned a lot this year. It's a pretty great opportunity. Of course, I'd rather be in the playoffs and be planning for whoever we're going to play, but we're not. So, what can we learn from it and figure out how do we get to where we want to get as quick as we can." Horst also addressed whether he required an extension commitment from Antetokounmpo before rebuilding the roster. "I trust and believe what Giannis has said and has been written: 'I want to win, and if I can win in Milwaukee that's where I want to be,'" Horst said. "But 100 percent believe that Giannis Antetokounmpo cares about winning and would love to win in Milwaukee. I believe that." Despite the uncertainty, Horst expressed confidence that both paths - retaining or trading the franchise cornerstone - remain achievable options as Milwaukee's front office begins its offseason evaluation.

title: Bulls Prioritize Retaining Billy Donovan As Part Of Next GM Search
summary: Chicago Bulls CEO Michael Reinsdorf announced Tuesday that any candidate for the team's vacant basketball operations position must be committed to retaining head coach Billy Donovan, a firm condition as the franchise begins a formal leadership search. Speaking on a video call one day after firing vice president Arturas Karnisovas and general manager Marc Eversley , Reinsdorf outlined his priorities for the rebuild. "If I interview someone and they're not sold on Billy, they're not sold on a Hall of Fame coach," Reinsdorf said. "If Billy wants to be our coach and someone's not interested in that, then they're probably not the right candidate for us." Donovan is set to meet with ownership the day after Sunday's season finale to discuss his future. Reinsdorf said he expected the conversation to be brief and expressed interest in expanding Donovan's role in personnel decisions. Donovan was considered a candidate for the University of North Carolina job that eventually went to Michael Malone. According to Jake Fischer , Donovan refused to interview for the Tar Heels' job. If Donovan becomes available, several NBA teams would be expected to pursue him. The Bulls, currently 30-49 with less than one week remaining in the regular season, will engage a search firm, something the organization did not do when hiring Karnisovas in 2020. Reinsdorf noted he never met Karnisovas in person before extending that offer. Reinsdorf identified clear priorities for the incoming executive: strong communication skills, process-oriented thinking, and a willingness to act decisively. He also directly criticized both tanking and short-term roster construction. "Going forward, it's about sustainability," Reinsdorf said. "I don't want to be just good for one or two years. I want it to be year in and year out, we have a chance to be competitive and win." Chicago holds notable assets heading into the offseason, including cap space and potential lottery selections, among them a conditional first-round pick from the Portland Trail Blazers if they qualify for the playoffs .

title: Wizards Clinch Keeping 2026 First Round Pick
summary: The Washington Wizards have accomplished their primary goal of this season: they'll keep their 2026 first-round draft pick. title: NBA's Anti-Tanking Proposals Not Embraced By Teams
summary: The NBA, led by commissioner Adam Silver, is determined to institute reforms to curb tanking. NBA teams, who will ultimately have to vote on any changes to the draft lottery system, are less sure about the changes. At a recent Board of Governors meeting, the NBA present three anti-tanking proposals. None of the three proposals were reportedly embraced with much warmth around the NBA. To be clear, these ideas weren't presented as "You must pick one" options. They were starting points for where the league could turn. While Silver would like to have new rules in place ahead of the start of next season, there's not even consensus among teams that tanking is a problem. Several teams agree that bottoming out and building through the draft is the best way to set your team up for future success. On the other side, several teams think that tanking creates a culture of losing that can be harmful to your franchise. Either way, Silver says things need to change for the NBA to continue to be successful. "I do think ultimately this is a decision that needs to be made at the ownership level," Silver said. "It has business implications, has basketball implications, has integrity implications for the league."

Redick said on Saturday that Reaves required two MRI scans to reveal a Grade 2 left oblique strain. "I don't know where the chain of command lies with Dallas imaging, but they scanned the wrong area," Redick said. "So [the mistake was] not on our end. We made it explicit what was supposed to be scanned, but they scanned the wrong area." Redick backed off the accusations when discussing the topic on Tuesday. "In the end, we got the image we needed," Redick said. "And obviously very appreciative, because it's happened throughout the season whenever the home team is accommodating to us, just like we would be for them." The Lakers still have not announced a timetable for Reaves' return.

title: Bulls Engage Search Firm To Fill Basketball Operations Vacancy
summary: The Chicago Bulls plan to hire a search firm to identify a new head of basketball operations, sources told ESPN. The team dismissed vice president Arturas Karnisovas and general manager Marc Eversley after six seasons leading the organization. Team president and CEO Michael Reinsdorf signaled a deliberate change in approach from the 2020 process that landed Karnisovas. "I think this process is going to be very different," Reinsdorf said. Karnisovas and Eversley compiled a 224-254 record over six years, producing one winning season and one playoff appearance. This marks only the second leadership change in basketball operations since 2003, when John Paxson held the role for 17 consecutive years before Karnisovas took over as he also shared duties with Gar Forman for parts of those years. Despite the team's poor record, the opening is considered an appealing opportunity. The Bulls hold their own lottery pick, roughly $60 million in projected cap space and several young players on team-friendly contracts. Josh Giddey and Matas Buzelis are Chicago's two foundational young players. Billy Donovan is expected to be retained in some capacity following the season's conclusion.

title: Michael Malone Signs Six-Year, $50M Deal With North Carolina
summary: North Carolina has officially hired former Denver Nuggets head coach Michael Malone, agreeing to a six-year contract worth approximately $50 million while averaging more than $8.3 million per year, sources confirmed to ESPN. The figure places Malone among the five highest-paid coaches in college basketball. Steve Kerr is currently the NBA's highest paid head coach at $17.5 million per season , but an $8.3 million salary would put Malone in the top-10 of NBA coaches ahead of JJ Redick at $8 million. "Carolina is one of the most historic programs in college basketball, and I am honored to be the head coach of the Tar Heels," Malone said in a statement. "I will do everything I can to continue UNC's championship legacy while preparing our players for professional careers and life after basketball." Malone, 52, compiled a 471-327 record across 10 seasons in Denver and led the Nuggets to the franchise's only NBA title in 2023. He was dismissed last April and joined ESPN as an analyst the following month. He has not coached at the college level since serving as a Manhattan assistant in 2001. Malone was recommended by a search committee led by executive associate athletic director Steve Newmark and director of athletics Bubba Cunningham. Malone had new ties to the Tar Heels as his daughter is on the volleyball team. UNC had pursued several prominent college coaches, including Michigan's Dusty May, Arizona's Tommy Lloyd and Iowa State's T.J. Otzelberger, before all three publicly committed to remaining at their current programs. "[Malone] is a brilliant coach who will deliver a modern and disciplined approach to leading our men's basketball program," executive associate athletic director Steve Newmark said. Malone inherits a program in transition. Former coach Hubert Davis was dismissed March 24, five days after the Tar Heels surrendered a 19-point lead in a first-round NCAA tournament loss to 11th-seeded VCU.
